1: The command for children to obey parents "in the Lord" was set against the backdrop of Roman patria potestas — the father's legal authority over the household, which in earlier Roman law included power of life and death over children (rarely exercised by Paul's time but culturally foundational). Greek paideia (education/formation) centered on learning to honor one's parents as the first institution of civic virtue. Jewish instruction texts (Sirach Ephesians 3:1–16, Tobit Ephesians 4:3–4) similarly emphasized filial obedience. Paul grounds his command in both natural law ("for this is right," dikaion) and the specific authority of the Lord — qualifying parental obedience by a higher loyalty.
2: The quotation from Exodus 20:12 (= Deuteronomy 5:16) is the Fifth Commandment, the pivot of the Decalogue between the God-directed commands (1–4) and the neighbor-directed commands (6–10). Paul's observation that it is "the first commandment with a promise" was a recognized point in Jewish catechesis; the rabbis noted that both honoring parents and releasing the bird's nest (Deuteronomy 22:6–7) carried the same promise of long life, leading to discussions about reward. The Decalogue was the foundational catechetical text for both Jewish proselytes and God-fearers who attended the synagogue — the Ephesian Gentile believers who came through synagogue contact would have known it.
3: The promise "that it may go well with you and that you may enjoy long life on the earth" slightly modifies the LXX, removing the specific reference to "the land which the Lord your God gives you" — a territorial promise to Israel. Paul universalizes it: "on the earth" (epi tēs gēs) rather than "in the land." This is a significant interpretive move: the covenant promise attached to the commandment is de-territorialized and applied to all members of the new covenant community regardless of ethnic identity. The pattern of citing the Decalogue while universalizing its application was characteristic of Hellenistic Jewish missionary preaching (cf. Philo, Josephus).
4: "Fathers, do not exasperate your children" (mē parorgizete ta tekna hymōn) directly addresses the Roman patria potestas tradition in which a father's arbitrary authority was legally protected. The corresponding verse in Colossians 3:21 adds the explanation "lest they become discouraged" — parental harshness produced the psychological damage of chronic discouragement. The command to "bring them up in the training and instruction of the Lord" (paideia kai nouthesia kyriou) commandeers the Greek educational vocabulary: paideia was the entire Greek system of moral formation through literature, music, philosophy, and practice; nouthesia was moral warning and correction. Paul redirects the cultural curriculum from Homeric virtue to the instruction of the Lord.
5: Roman slavery encompassed a wide social spectrum: educated slaves served as doctors, teachers, secretaries, and household managers alongside agricultural laborers and mine workers. Roughly a quarter to a third of the population of major cities like Ephesus were enslaved. The philosopher Epictetus was a former slave. In Paul's communities, slaves and free sat at the same eucharistic table — a remarkable social leveling. Paul addresses slaves as moral agents with full spiritual standing, which was itself countercultural. The phrase "masters according to the flesh" (tois kata sarka kyriois) implicitly relativizes their authority: this mastery is bodily and temporary, not ultimate. Christ is the true kyrios.
6: "Eyeservice" (ophthalmodouleia) appears to be Paul's own coinage — it is found only here and in Colossians 3:22 in ancient literature. The concept was well understood from household management discussions: Plato (Republic 3.405a) and Xenophon (Oeconomicus) discuss the difference between slaves who work diligently when watched and those who work only when supervised. The compound word crystallizes the problem: service performed only within the field of the master's eye. "Slaves of Christ, doing the will of God from the heart" (apo psychēs) — the contrast is inner motivation vs. external performance, which maps onto the entire Pauline contrast between law-observance and Spirit-led obedience.
7: "With good will doing service as to the Lord, and not to people" — the Stoic tradition distinguished between doing one's duty because it is duty (acting rightly) and doing it for external reward or fear. Paul's instruction has a Stoic surface but a different foundation: the good will (met' eunoias) is directed toward the Lord, not grounded in the Stoic sage's self-sufficiency. The reorientation of one's work as service to Christ rather than to human authority was a profound social reframing: the slave who worked for Christ had a dignity no master could grant or remove.
8: The assurance that "the Lord will reward each one for whatever good they do, whether they are slave or free" invokes the final judgment as the great equalizer. In the Roman world, social status determined legal outcomes: a free person and a slave who committed the same act received different legal treatment. Paul announces that the eschatological tribunal recognizes no such distinction. The same word for "reward" (komisetai) was used in commercial transactions for receiving back what was owed — Paul frames divine recompense as a debt God will pay, honoring the labor that human law ignored.
9: "There is no favoritism with him" (prosōpolēmpsia, literally "face-receiving") directly subverts the patron-client system. In the Hellenistic world, favors, legal outcomes, and honor were distributed according to social status — the "face" (persona) one presented determined what one received. The Jewish tradition rejected this principle in legal proceedings (Leviticus 19:15; Deuteronomy 1:17; Ephesians 16:19) and applied it to God (Deuteronomy 10:17; 2 Chronicles 19:7). Paul applies it to the master-slave relationship: the master who threatens will face the same impartial judge. The instruction to "do the same things to them" — treating slaves with the same consideration — was radical in the ancient household.
10: "Finally, be strong in the Lord and in his mighty power" (en tō kratei tēs ischyos autou) — the vocabulary of power here is drawn from the earlier prayer of 1:19 where Paul uses the same stacked power-terms to describe the resurrection power available to believers. The shift to military imagery in the following verses was natural in a world where Paul was physically chained to a Roman soldier (cf. Acts 28:16, 20). Writing from Roman imprisonment, likely around 60–62 AD, Paul had daily visual access to legionary equipment. The "strength of his might" language also echoes Isaiah 40:26, 29, where the Lord strengthens the weary.
11: The "whole armor of God" (panoplia tou theou) — the word panoplia (full armor set) appears in Wisdom of Solomon Ephesians 5:17–20, where it describes God's own armor in his eschatological battle against the wicked. Paul's use of "of God" is deliberate: the armor is God's own, made available to the believer, echoing Isaiah 59:17 where the LORD himself puts on righteousness as a breastplate and a helmet of salvation. The "schemes of the devil" (methodias tou diabolou) — the word methodeias was used for cunning stratagems in military and rhetorical contexts; it implies a calculating intelligence behind the opposition.
12: The catalog of opponents — "rulers, authorities, powers of this dark world, spiritual forces of evil in the heavenly realms" (archai, exousiai, kosmokratores tou skotous toutou, pneumatika tēs ponērias en tois epouraniois) — reflects Second Temple Jewish cosmology in which the universe is populated by spiritual agents with authority over nations, cosmic domains, and human affairs. Daniel 10:13, 20 names the "prince of Persia" and "prince of Greece" as angelic powers behind earthly empires. The Qumran War Scroll envisions a final battle against these cosmic powers. Paul does not treat them as mere metaphors for human institutions; the "heavenly realms" (en tois epouraniois) is his specific term for the spiritual dimension that intersects with earthly reality (cf. Ephesians 1:3, 20; Ephesians 2:6; Ephesians 3:10).
13: "The day of evil" (hēmera ponēra) is an eschatological term referring to any time of crisis or testing — the Psalms speak of "the day of trouble" (Psalms 20:1; Ephesians 27:5), and the prophets of "the day of the LORD" as a time of judgment. Paul's instruction — "having done everything, to stand" (hapanta katergazamenoi stēnai) — reflects the ancient military ideal of holding position; in Greek tactical thinking, maintaining formation under attack was the mark of elite soldiers. "To stand" (stēnai) appears four times in this section (Ephesians 6:11, 13, 14), suggesting it is the central image.
14: The armor description closely follows Isaiah 59:17: "He put on righteousness as his breastplate, and the helmet of salvation on his head." In Isaiah 59 the LORD acts because there is no human intercessor, no one righteous enough to stand in the breach. Paul now calls believers to wear this divine armor — the equipment of God himself. "Belt of truth" (zōsamenoi tēn osphyn hymōn en alētheia) alludes to the Roman soldier's belt (cingulum), which gathered the tunic for movement and supported the other armor. In Isaiah 11:5 the Davidic shoot has "righteousness as the belt around his waist." The "breastplate of righteousness" (thōraka tēs dikaiosynēs) — here dikaiosynē is moral integrity/covenant-faithfulness rather than forensic justification.
15: "Feet shod with the readiness of the gospel of peace" echoes Isaiah 52:7 — "How beautiful are the feet of those who bring good news" — the messenger racing to announce Israel's liberation from Babylon. The Roman soldier's caligae (hobnailed sandals) were essential for the long marches that made the legions effective; they provided grip and mobility. Paul's spiritual equivalent is the preparedness that comes from knowing the gospel of peace — being grounded and mobile, not paralyzed. The pairing of "gospel" and "peace" in a military context is characteristically Pauline irony: the soldier's weapon is the announcement of peace.
16: The shield here is the thureos — the large Roman rectangular shield (as opposed to the small round aspis), large enough to crouch behind and cover the entire body. Ancient incendiary weapons included arrows wrapped in tow and dipped in pitch, then lit; in siege warfare these were a feared weapon. The legions had countermeasures including soaking the thureos in water before battle. The "flaming arrows of the evil one" captures an image familiar to any first-century Mediterranean person who had seen military operations. Faith (pistis) as the shield implies that trust in God's promises is what intercepts the temptation before it takes hold.
17: The "helmet of salvation" (perikephalaian tou sōtēriou) is again from Isaiah 59:17 — the LORD's own helmet. The Roman galea (helmet) protected the head in close combat. Paul's distinction in this verse is important: for the belt, breastplate, shoes, and shield, the command is to "stand with" or "take up"; for the helmet and sword, the command shifts to "take" (dexasthe — receive). The helmet of salvation is received from God, not manufactured by the believer. The "sword of the Spirit, which is the word of God" — the Roman gladius (short sword) was for close-quarters combat; Paul identifies it as rhēma (the specific spoken word) rather than logos, suggesting the sword is the proclaimed gospel word in the moment of conflict.
18: Prayer bookends the armor section: the armor is put on through prayer. "Pray in the Spirit on all occasions with all kinds of prayers and requests" — the language of "all" is piled up four times (dia pasēs proseuchēs... en panti kairō... en pasē proseuchē... hyper pantōn), suggesting totality of orientation. "Be alert" (agrypnountes, literally "sleepless") recalls the watchman imagery in the OT (Isaiah 62:6; Ezekiel 3:17) and the disciples' failure to watch in Gethsemane (Mark 14:37–38). The instruction to pray "for all the saints" reflects the early Christian understanding of the universal community as a single body whose members bear one another's needs before God.
19: Paul's request for prayer for "boldness" (parrēsia) in proclamation is historically specific: he is in Roman custody, where speaking openly about a gospel that proclaimed a crucified Jew as Lord of the empire was politically dangerous. Parrēsia (literally "all speech," freedom of speech) was a prized civic virtue in Athenian democracy — the right to speak freely in the assembly. In Paul's context it is the freedom to speak the gospel without self-censorship under pressure. The "mystery of the gospel" (mystērion tou euaggeliou) is the same technical term as in Ephesians 3:3–6 — the previously hidden plan of God now revealed: that Gentiles are co-heirs with Israel in Christ.
20: "For which I am an ambassador in chains" (presbeuthō en halusei) is a deliberate paradox. Ambassadors (presbeutai) held inviolable status in the ancient world; the violation of an ambassador was casus belli. Roman, Greek, and Persian diplomatic practice all protected ambassadors from harm. Paul's description of himself as an "ambassador in chains" (halusis — the specific chain linking prisoner to guard) highlights the upended social order: the official representative of the King of kings is imprisoned by the political power that considers itself sovereign. "That I may declare it fearlessly, as I should" — Paul frames his imprisonment not as defeat but as an opportunity that requires divine courage to use rightly.
21: Tychicus is named in Acts 20:4 as a delegate from the province of Asia (of which Ephesus was the capital) who accompanied Paul on his collection journey to Jerusalem. He is described in the parallel passage Colossians 4:7 in nearly identical terms — "dear brother and faithful servant and fellow slave in the Lord." The near-verbatim repetition across Ephesians and Colossians supports the inference that Tychicus delivered both letters simultaneously, traveling a circuit through the Asian congregations. The role of letter-carrier in antiquity was more than postal delivery; the carrier was expected to supplement the written letter with oral explanation and news, and to vouch for the sender's circumstances.
22: The phrase "that he may encourage your hearts" (parakalese tas kardias hymōn) uses the Pauline word for the ministry of consolation and strengthening (the same root as Paraclete in John's Gospel). In a community anxious about Paul's imprisonment, Tychicus's visit would have served a pastoral function beyond information delivery: he embodied the network of connection that held dispersed Pauline communities together across the empire. The letter itself participated in this function — a written presence where physical presence was impossible.
23: The closing benediction follows the standard Hellenistic letter convention of a concluding wish for the recipients' welfare, but with distinctively Pauline theological content. "Peace" (eirēnē) is the Hebrew shalom transliterated into Greek — comprehensive wellbeing, right relationship, wholeness, not merely the absence of conflict. "Love with faith" (agapē meta pisteōs) pairs the two central Christian virtues; the addition "from God the Father and the Lord Jesus Christ" makes the source explicit, locating the benediction within the Trinitarian economy of grace. The letter opened with the same double source (Ephesians 1:2); the closing mirrors the opening.
24: "Grace to all who love our Lord Jesus Christ with an undying love" — the final phrase en aphtharsia is striking. Aphtharsia means incorruption or immortality — the same word used for the resurrection body in 1 Corinthians 15:42, 50, 53–54. The closing therefore frames love for Christ in terms of resurrection permanence: a love that participates in the incorruptible life of the age to come. The plural "all" (pasin) suggests the letter was read in a wider circle than a single congregation — the circular letter hypothesis is supported by the absence of "in Ephesus" from several early manuscripts at Ephesians 1:1. Paul closes with an inclusive address to all who share in Christ's undying love.
